original_abstract: Members of nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) have been critical of the Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) initiative in Afghanistan since its inception, claiming that the mixture of military and humanitarian operations has resulted in ‘blurred lines’ that inhibit insurgents from identifying who is and is not a combatant. Certain organizations have hypothesized that aid workers are more likely to come under attack as a result of this mixture. Although this claim has surfaced in multiple outlets over the years, there was a lack of empirical evidence to support it. This study tests this hypothesis using a panel-corrected standard error regression model of all 34 Afghan provinces in 2010 and 2011. Preliminary results show that NGOs were likely to encounter a greater number of security incidents in provinces with PRTs; however, further analysis reveals this was only the case in provinces with teams not led by the US. This calls into question the validity of a general ‘blurred lines’ explanation for decreased aid worker security.
humanized_abstract: The PRT concept has been heavily criticized by NGOs staff in Afghanistan since the very beginning. They assert that the presence of a military component in a supposedly aid-related endeavour has the effect of confusing the insurgency, and thereby preventing it from distinguishing between military and non-military personnel. Some media outlets have reiterated on numerous occasions that, as a result, aid personnel would probably be more exposed to attacks. As this assertion has been done time and again in the press without much empirical underpinning, this research aimed at empirically checking the “exposure” to attacks in the case of NGOs staff in provinces where a PRT was stationed. The research applies a panel-corrected standard error regression model for all 34 Afghan provinces for 2010 and 2011. Preliminary findings suggest that there was a statistically significant presence of attacks on NGOs in PRT stations. Yet, further investigation shows that this was the case in PRTs commanded by any military beside the U.S. Army, therefore questioning the applicability of the “blurred lines” argument, and the assertion that aid personnel were likely to be attacked as a result of the military integration of development assistance in Afghanistan.
